title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Technical Report)
text:
Loading Packages
We load the following R packages using the pacman::p_load() function:

tidyverse for data manipulation and visualization
ggplot for data visualization
readxl for reading Excel, CSV files
skimr for data profiling
patchwork for preparing composite figure created using ggplot2
ggstatsplot for creating graphics with details from statistical tests included in the information-rich plots themselves
ggdist for visualizations of distributions and uncertainty
ggridges for ridgeline plots
scales for providing the internal scaling infrastructure used by ggplot2
viridis for providing colorblind-friendly, perceptually uniform color palettes


pacman::p_load(tidyverse, ggplot2, readxl, skimr, patchwork, ggstatsplot, ggdist, ggridges, scales, viridis)

Data Pre-Processing
After doing data exploration, we can quickly see some issues with our dataset.
Missing Value Treatment:

fuel_type: 1,287 missing values (0.2%) removed due to neglibile impact.
age_driving_licence: 2 missing values (<0.001%) removed due to negligible impact
vehicle_age: 2 missing values (<0.001%) removed due to negligible impact
vehicle_value: 513 missing values (0.08%) removed due to negligible impact

Features Engineering:

profitability: total_premium - total_incurred
profit_ratio: profitability / total_premium
has_claim: “Yes” if total_incurred > 0, “No” otherwise
years_with_licence: year - age_driving_licence (actual years of driving experience)

Data Cleaning:

We will filter rows with null profitability. This is due to null total_premium and/or total_incurred.
We will filter rows with null age_driving_licence.


insurance_clean <- insurance_data %>%
  mutate(
    years_with_licence = year - age_driving_licence,
    profitability = total_premium - total_incurred,
    profit_ratio = ifelse(total_premium > 0, profitability / total_premium, NA),
    has_claim = ifelse(total_incurred > 0, "Yes", "No")
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(profitability), !is.na(age_driving_licence), !is.na(vehicle_age), !is.na(fuel_type), !is.na(vehicle_value))

cat("Original records:", nrow(insurance_data), "\n")

Original records: 354140 

cat("After cleaning:", nrow(insurance_clean), "\n")

After cleaning: 352338 

cat("Records removed:", nrow(insurance_data) - nrow(insurance_clean), "\n")

Records removed: 1802 




Distribution Assessment and Statistical Test Selection
Before conducting statistical tests, we assess the normality of key numerical variables to determine whether parametric or non-parametric tests are appropriate.

Q-Q Plots for Normality Assessment

p_qq1 <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(sample = total_premium)) +
  stat_qq(alpha = 0.4) +
  stat_qq_line(color = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "Q-Q Plot: Premium",
    x = "Theoretical Quantiles",
    y = "Sample Quantiles"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p_qq2 <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(sample = total_incurred)) +
  stat_qq(alpha = 0.4) +
  stat_qq_line(color = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "Q-Q Plot: Claims",
    x = "Theoretical Quantiles",
    y = "Sample Quantiles"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p_qq3 <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(sample = profitability)) +
  stat_qq(alpha = 0.4) +
  stat_qq_line(color = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "Q-Q Plot: Profitability",
    x = "Theoretical Quantiles",
    y = "Sample Quantiles"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

(p_qq1 | p_qq2) / p_qq3








Interpretation: The Q-Q plots show clear deviations from the theoretical normal line for all three variables. total_premium displays right-skewness, while total_incurred has a very heavy right tail due to large claim observations. profitability shows strong left-tail deviation, suggesting the presence of extreme negative profitability cases. Therefore, the normality assumption is not satisfied, and non-parametric tests such as the Mann-Whitney U test and Kruskal-Wallis test are more appropriate for subsequent group comparisons.



Visual Analytics Approach
This analysis employs a systematic visual analytics strategy to explore the insurance portfolio data:
Univariate Analysis:

Histograms with density curves to reveal distribution shapes, identify skewness, and detect multimodality
Boxplots to identify outliers, visualize quartile ranges, and assess data spread
Log transformations to demonstrate data transformation techniques for handling skewed distributions and to show how transformations affect interpretability

Bivariate Analysis:

Scatter plots with correlation coefficients to assess the strength and direction of relationships between continuous variables
Grouped bar charts to compare mean values across categorical groups
Ridgeline plots (from ggridges package) to compare full distributions across multiple groups simultaneously, showing both central tendency and spread
Raincloud plots (from ggdist package) to combine density curves, boxplots, and individual data points in a single visualization, providing comprehensive distributional information
Error bar plots with confidence intervals to quantify uncertainty in estimates and enable visual assessment of statistical significance

Statistical Validation:

ggbetweenstats (from ggstatsplot package) to combine visualization with statistical testing, providing p-values, effect sizes, and pairwise comparisons in a single plot
Non-parametric tests (Kruskal-Wallis, Mann-Whitney) chosen based on Q-Q plot assessment showing non-normal distributions

This multi-layered approach ensures that findings are both visually interpretable and statistically validated, supporting evidence-based recommendations for portfolio management.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Technical Report)
text:
Part 1: Univariate Visual Analytics

Numerical Variables

Distribution of Premium

p1 <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(x = total_premium)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                 bins = 50, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_density(color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Premium",
    x = "Premium (€)",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2 <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(y = total_premium)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Boxplot of Premium",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Premium (€)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p1 / p2








Interpretation: The distribution of total premium is right-skewed, with most policies having relatively low premium values. The histogram shows that the majority of observations are concentrated below €500, while a small number of policies have much higher premiums, extending beyond €5,000. The boxplot confirms the presence of several upper-tail outliers. Therefore, the premium variable is not normally distributed. Will apply log transformation to this column to make a better chart only.

ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(x = log1p(total_premium))) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                 bins = 50, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_density(color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Premium after Log Transformation",
    x = "log(1 + Premium)",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()










Distribution of Claims

p3 <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(x = total_incurred)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 50, fill = "coral", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_density(color = "darkred", linewidth = 1) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Claim Costs", x = "Claim Cost (€)", y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal()

p4 <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(x = total_incurred)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "coral", alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(title = "Boxplot of Claim Costs", x = "Claim Cost (€)") +
  theme_minimal()

p3 / p4








Interpretation: The distribution of total incurred claim costs is extremely right-skewed. Most policies have very low or zero claim costs, while a small number of observations have exceptionally large claim amounts, reaching nearly €600,000. The boxplot confirms the presence of many upper-tail outliers. This pattern is common in insurance data, where most policyholders do not make large claims, but a few severe claims can significantly affect the overall distribution. Therefore, the variable is not normally distributed, and log transformation or non-parametric methods may be more appropriate for further analysis.

insurance_clean <- insurance_clean %>%
  mutate(log_total_incurred = log1p(total_incurred))

p3_log <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(x = log_total_incurred)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                 bins = 50, fill = "coral", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_density(color = "darkred", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Claim Costs after Log Transformation",
    x = "log(1 + Claim Cost)",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p4_log <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(y = log_total_incurred)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "coral", alpha = 0.7) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Boxplot of Claim Costs after Log Transformation",
    x = NULL,
    y = "log(1 + Claim Cost)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p3_log / p4_log








Interpretation: After applying the log transformation, the distribution of claim costs becomes easier to inspect, but a large spike remains at zero. This indicates that many policies have no incurred claims. Among policies with positive claim costs, the distribution is still right-skewed, although the log transformation reduces the influence of extreme claim values.
Lets take a look at the distribution of positive claims only.

claims_positive <- insurance_clean %>%
  filter(total_incurred > 0) %>%
  mutate(log_total_incurred = log(total_incurred))

p3_log_positive <- ggplot(claims_positive, aes(x = log_total_incurred)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                 bins = 50, fill = "coral", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_density(color = "darkred", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Positive Claim Costs after Log Transformation",
    subtitle = "Only policies with total incurred claims greater than zero are included",
    x = "log(Claim Cost)",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p4_log_positive <- ggplot(claims_positive, aes(y = log_total_incurred)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "coral", alpha = 0.7) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Boxplot of Positive Claim Costs after Log Transformation",
    x = NULL,
    y = "log(Claim Cost)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p3_log_positive / p4_log_positive








Interpretation: Although only positive incurred claims were included, the presence of extremely low log values suggests that some positive claim costs are very close to zero. These observations should be inspected because they may reflect very small claim payments, rounding effects, or possible data issues. Therefore, while the log transformation helps reduce the impact of very large claim costs, it does not fully remove extreme observations.
The distribution remains non-normal even after log transformation, so non-parametric or robust methods remain appropriate for comparing claim costs across groups.


Distribution of Profitability

p5 <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(x = profitability)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                 bins = 50, fill = "forestgreen", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_density(color = "darkgreen", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Profitability",
    subtitle = "Profitability = Premium - Claims",
    x = "Profitability (€)",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p6 <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(y = profitability)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "forestgreen", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Boxplot of Profitability",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Profitability (€)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p5 / p6








Interpretation: Profitability, defined as total premium minus total incurred claims, shows a highly left-skewed distribution. Most policies have profitability close to zero or moderately positive, while a small number of policies generate very large losses due to high claim costs. The vertical dashed line at zero separates profitable and loss-making policies. The long negative tail indicates that claim severity has a strong impact on insurer profitability. Therefore, profitability is not normally distributed, and non-parametric methods or transformed visualizations are more appropriate for further analysis.

insurance_clean <- insurance_clean %>%
  mutate(signed_log_profitability = sign(profitability) * log1p(abs(profitability)))

ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(x = signed_log_profitability)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                 bins = 50, fill = "forestgreen", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_density(color = "darkgreen", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Profitability after Signed Log Transformation",
    x = "sign(profitability) × log(1 + |profitability|)",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()








Interpretation: The transformed distribution shows two distinct regions: a large cluster of profitable policies and a smaller group of loss-making policies. Most observations are concentrated on the positive side of zero, while the negative tail indicates policies where incurred claims exceed premiums. This confirms that profitability is not normally distributed and supports the use of non-parametric methods in subsequent analyses.


Ridgeline Plot: Profitability by Fuel Type

library(ggridges)
library(scales)

insurance_clean %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = profitability, y = fuel_type, fill = fuel_type)) +
  geom_density_ridges(scale = 0.9, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-2000, 2000)) +
  labs(
    title = "Profitability Distribution by Fuel Type",
    subtitle = "Ridgeline plot showing distribution overlap and differences",
    x = "Profitability (€)",
    y = "Fuel Type"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")








Interpretation: The ridgeline plot compares the profitability distributions across different fuel types. Overall, the distributions largely overlap, suggesting that profitability patterns are broadly similar across fuel categories. Most policies are concentrated around slightly positive profitability values, while each fuel type also shows some negative-profitability cases. Diesel and gasoline vehicles appear to have similar central distributions.
The below plot is restricted to the central profitability range to improve readability because extreme loss values distort the density estimation.

library(ggridges)
library(scales)
library(dplyr)

insurance_clean %>%
  filter(profitability >= -2000, profitability <= 2000) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = profitability, y = fuel_type, fill = fuel_type)) +
  geom_density_ridges(scale = 0.9, alpha = 0.7, rel_min_height = 0.01) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Profitability Distribution by Fuel Type",
    subtitle = "Zoomed view: profitability between -€2,000 and €2,000",
    x = "Profitability (€)",
    y = "Fuel Type"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")










Distribution of Vehicle Age

p_vage1 <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(x = vehicle_age)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 30, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_density(color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Vehicle Age", x = "Vehicle Age (years)", y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal()

p_vage2 <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(y = vehicle_age)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Boxplot of Vehicle Age", x = NULL, y = "Vehicle Age (years)") +
  theme_minimal()

p_vage1 / p_vage2








Interpretation: The distribution of vehicle_age is not uniform. Most vehicles are concentrated between approximately 10 and 45 years old, with the highest density around 15–30 years. There are relatively fewer very new vehicles and fewer vehicles older than 50 years. Based on this distribution, vehicle_age was grouped into wider age bands: 0-5, 6-10, 11-20, 21-30, 31-40, 41-50, and 50+ years. This binning approach improves interpretability while preserving the main structure of the original distribution.

insurance_clean <- insurance_clean %>%
  mutate(
    vehicle_age_group = case_when(
      vehicle_age < 0 ~ "Invalid",
      vehicle_age <= 5 ~ "0-5 years",
      vehicle_age <= 10 ~ "6-10 years",
      vehicle_age <= 20 ~ "11-20 years",
      vehicle_age <= 30 ~ "21-30 years",
      vehicle_age <= 40 ~ "31-40 years",
      vehicle_age <= 50 ~ "41-50 years",
      TRUE ~ "50+ years"
    ),
    vehicle_age_group = factor(
      vehicle_age_group,
      levels = c(
        "Invalid", "0-5 years", "6-10 years", "11-20 years",
        "21-30 years", "31-40 years", "41-50 years", "50+ years"
      )
    )
  )



Distribution of Driver Age

p_dage1 <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(x = driver_age)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 30, fill = "coral", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_density(color = "darkred", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Driver Age", x = "Driver Age (years)", y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal()

p_dage2 <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(y = driver_age)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "coral", alpha = 0.7) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Boxplot of Driver Age", x = NULL, y = "Driver Age (years)") +
  theme_minimal()

p_dage1 / p_dage2








Interpretation: From the distribution, most drivers are between around 35 and 65 years old, with the highest density around the late 30s to early 50s. The distribution is slightly right-skewed, with a small number of older drivers above 80 appearing as outliers in the boxplot. Since age may have a non-linear relationship with profitability and claim risk, grouping driver age into age bands makes the analysis easier to interpret.

insurance_clean <- insurance_clean %>%
  mutate(
    driver_age_group = case_when(
      driver_age <= 25 ~ "18-25 years",
      driver_age <= 35 ~ "26-35 years",
      driver_age <= 50 ~ "36-50 years",
      driver_age <= 65 ~ "51-65 years",
      TRUE ~ "65+ years"
    ),
    driver_age_group = factor(
      driver_age_group,
      levels = c("18-25 years", "26-35 years", "36-50 years", "51-65 years", "65+ years")
    )
  )

This grouping improves interpretability and allows profitability patterns to be compared across different driver age segments.


Distribution of Driving License Age
The variable years_with_licence appears to represent the calendar year in which the driver obtained their licence, rather than the number of years of driving experience. Since the dataset covers policies from 2022 to 2024, this variable was transformed into age_driving_licence by subtracting the licence year from the policy year. This derived variable better represents driving experience and is more interpretable for analysing claim risk and profitability.

insurance_clean <- insurance_clean %>%
  mutate(
    age_driving_licence = 2024 - years_with_licence
  )

p_lage1 <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(x = age_driving_licence)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 30, fill = "forestgreen", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_density(color = "darkgreen", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Driving Experience",
    x = "Years Since Licence Obtained",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p_lage2 <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(y = age_driving_licence)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "forestgreen", alpha = 0.7) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Boxplot of Driving Experience",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Years Since Licence Obtained"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p_lage1 / p_lage2








Interpretation: The distribution of driving experience is highly right-skewed. Most drivers have around 18 to 25 years since obtaining their licence, with the highest concentration close to 20 years. The boxplot also shows several outliers, especially among drivers with more than 40 years of experience, and a few very low values close to zero. This suggests that the variable is not evenly distributed and may have a non-linear relationship with profitability or claim risk. Therefore, driving experience was converted into grouped categories to make the analysis easier to interpret and reduce the influence of individual extreme values.
Based on this distribution, driving experience was grouped into 0-10, 11-20, 21-30, 31-40, and 40+ years. Wider groups were used because most observations are concentrated around 18–25 years, while the higher-experience values form a long tail.

insurance_clean <- insurance_clean %>%
  mutate(
    driving_experience_group = case_when(
      age_driving_licence < 0 ~ "Invalid",
      age_driving_licence <= 10 ~ "0-10 years",
      age_driving_licence <= 20 ~ "11-20 years",
      age_driving_licence <= 30 ~ "21-30 years",
      age_driving_licence <= 40 ~ "31-40 years",
      TRUE ~ "40+ years"
    ),
    driving_experience_group = factor(
      driving_experience_group,
      levels = c(
        "Invalid", "0-10 years", "11-20 years",
        "21-30 years", "31-40 years", "40+ years"
      )
    )
  )



Distribution of Power-to-Weight Ratio

p_pwr1 <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(x = power_to_weight_ratio)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 30, fill = "purple", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_density(color = "darkviolet", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Weight-to-Power Ratio",
    x = "Weight-to-Power Ratio (kg per horsepower)",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p_pwr2 <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(y = power_to_weight_ratio)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "purple", alpha = 0.7) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Boxplot of Weight-to-Power Ratio",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Weight-to-Power Ratio (kg per horsepower)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p_pwr1 / p_pwr2








Interpretation: The distribution of weight-to-power ratio is strongly right-skewed. Most vehicles are concentrated around approximately 8 to 15 kg per horsepower, while a smaller number of vehicles have much higher ratios. The boxplot also shows several high-end outliers, including one extreme value above 60. Since this variable is measured as kg per horsepower, a lower value indicates stronger vehicle performance, while a higher value indicates lower performance relative to vehicle weight. Therefore, the variable was grouped into categories to improve interpretability and reduce the influence of individual extreme values.

insurance_clean <- insurance_clean %>%
  mutate(
    weight_power_group = cut(
      power_to_weight_ratio,
      breaks = quantile(
        power_to_weight_ratio,
        probs = c(0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1),
        na.rm = TRUE
      ),
      include.lowest = TRUE,
      labels = c("Low ratio / High performance",
                 "Medium-low ratio",
                 "Medium-high ratio",
                 "High ratio / Low performance")
    )
  )



Distribution of Total Exposure

p_exp1 <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(x = total_exposure)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 30, fill = "orange", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_density(color = "darkorange", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Total Exposure", x = "Total Exposure (years)", y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal()

p_exp2 <- ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(y = total_exposure)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "orange", alpha = 0.7) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Boxplot of Total Exposure", x = NULL, y = "Total Exposure (years)") +
  theme_minimal()

p_exp1 / p_exp2








Interpretation: The distribution of total_exposure shows that many policies have exposure close to 1 year, meaning they were active for almost the full policy period. However, there are also many partial-year policies with exposure values below 1. The boxplot shows that the median exposure is relatively high, around 0.8–0.9 years, while the lower quartile is around 0.4–0.5 years. Since exposure measures the amount of time a policy is active, it affects both premium earned and claim opportunity. Therefore, total_exposure should be considered when interpreting profitability and claim risk.
Because exposure is bounded between 0 and 1 and has a large concentration near 1, it was grouped into exposure bands to compare short-term and full-year policies more clearly.

insurance_clean <- insurance_clean %>%
  mutate(
    exposure_group = case_when(
      is.na(total_exposure) ~ NA_character_,
      total_exposure < 0.25 ~ "Very short exposure",
      total_exposure < 0.50 ~ "Short exposure",
      total_exposure < 0.75 ~ "Medium exposure",
      total_exposure < 1.00 ~ "High exposure",
      total_exposure >= 1.00 ~ "Full-year exposure",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ),
    exposure_group = factor(
      exposure_group,
      levels = c(
        "Very short exposure",
        "Short exposure",
        "Medium exposure",
        "High exposure",
        "Full-year exposure"
      )
    )
  )




Categorical Variables

Fuel Type Distribution

insurance_clean %>%
  count(fuel_type) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(fuel_type, n), y = n, fill = fuel_type)) +

    aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%")),

  ) +

  ) +

    title = "Distribution of Fuel Types",
    x = "Fuel Type",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")








Interpretation: The fuel type distribution is highly imbalanced. Diesel vehicles account for the majority of the portfolio, representing around 64.2% of policies, followed by gasoline vehicles at 35.8%.


Geographic Distribution

insurance_clean %>%
  count(circulation_area) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(circulation_area, n), y = n, fill = circulation_area)) +
  geom_col() +

    aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%")),

  ) +

  ) +

    x = "Area",

  ) +

Interpretation: Area U is slightly more common, but both R and U have substantial sample sizes.


Municipality Type Distribution

insurance_clean %>%
  count(municipality_type) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(municipality_type, n), y = n, fill = municipality_type)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%")), hjust = -0.1) +

Interpretation: The distribution of municipality_type shows that most policyholders are from inland municipalities, accounting for 64.6% of the dataset. Coastal municipalities represent 28.6%, while island municipalities account for only 6.8%. This indicates that the portfolio is geographically imbalanced, with inland areas dominating the sample. Since municipality type may reflect different driving environments, road conditions, traffic density, and claim exposure, it is useful to include this variable when comparing claim risk and profitability. However, results for the island group should be interpreted carefully because it has a much smaller sample size.
Recode the labels for readability only.

insurance_clean <- insurance_clean %>%
  mutate(
    municipality_type_label = case_when(
      municipality_type == "I" ~ "Inland",
      municipality_type == "C" ~ "Coastal",
      municipality_type == "IS" ~ "Islands",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ),
    municipality_type_label = factor(
      municipality_type_label,
      levels = c("Inland", "Coastal", "Islands", "Other")
    )
  )

insurance_clean %>%
  count(municipality_type_label) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(municipality_type_label, n), y = n, fill = municipality_type_label)) +

    title = "Distribution by Municipality Type",
    x = "Municipality Type",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +

Bonus Score Distribution

insurance_clean %>%
  count(bonus_score) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(bonus_score, n), y = n, fill = bonus_score)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%")), hjust = -0.1) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(title = "Distribution by Bonus Score", x = "Bonus Score", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")








Interpretation: The distribution of bonus_score is highly imbalanced. The majority of policyholders are classified as G, representing a favourable claims history, and this group accounts for 96.4% of the dataset. The N group, representing a neutral profile, accounts for only 2.2%, while the B group, representing a poor claims history, accounts for 1.3%. This suggests that most policyholders in the portfolio have a positive claims history. However, because the N and B groups are very small, any profitability or claim-risk comparison involving these groups should be interpreted carefully.
Recode the labels for readability.

insurance_clean <- insurance_clean %>%
  mutate(
    bonus_score_label = case_when(
      bonus_score == "G" ~ "Good claims history",
      bonus_score == "N" ~ "Neutral profile",
      bonus_score == "B" ~ "Poor claims history",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ),
    bonus_score_label = factor(
      bonus_score_label,
      levels = c("Good claims history", "Neutral profile", "Poor claims history", "Other")
    )
  )

insurance_clean %>%
  count(bonus_score_label) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(bonus_score_label, n), y = n, fill = bonus_score_label)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%")), hjust = -0.1) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(title = "Distribution by Bonus Score", x = "Bonus Score", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")










Policy Type Distribution

insurance_clean %>%
  count(policy_type) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(policy_type, n), y = n, fill = policy_type)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%")), hjust = -0.1) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(title = "Distribution by Policy Type", x = "Policy Type", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")








Interpretation: The distribution of policy_type shows that the portfolio is mainly concentrated in CC policies, which account for 57.9% of all observations. The second largest group is COMP_E, representing 25.6%, followed by TPG at 10.3%. In contrast, COMP_N and TP are much smaller groups, accounting for only 4.5% and 1.6%, respectively. This indicates that most policies include third-party liability combined with additional coverages, while basic third-party-only policies are relatively rare.
Since policy type represents the coverage structure of the insurance contract, it is expected to be closely related to premium, claim cost, and profitability. Policies with broader coverage may generate higher premiums but may also expose the insurer to more types of claims. Therefore, policy_type is an important variable for comparing profitability and claim risk across different insurance products.
However, the smaller sample sizes for TP and COMP_N mean that results for these groups should be interpreted carefully, as their profitability patterns may be less stable.
Recode the labels for readability.

insurance_clean <- insurance_clean %>%
  mutate(
    policy_type_label = case_when(
      policy_type == "TP" ~ "Third-party only",
      policy_type == "TPG" ~ "Third-party + glass",
      policy_type == "CC" ~ "Third-party + add-ons",
      policy_type == "COMP_E" ~ "Comprehensive with excess",
      policy_type == "COMP_N" ~ "Comprehensive without excess",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ),
    policy_type_label = factor(
      policy_type_label,
      levels = c(
        "Third-party only",
        "Third-party + glass",
        "Third-party + add-ons",
        "Comprehensive with excess",
        "Comprehensive without excess",
        "Other"
      )
    )
  )

insurance_clean %>%
  count(policy_type_label) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(policy_type_label, n), y = n, fill = policy_type_label)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%")), hjust = -0.1) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(title = "Distribution by Policy Type", x = "Policy Type", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")










Claims Occurrence

insurance_clean %>%
  count(has_claim) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = has_claim, y = n, fill = has_claim)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%\n(n=", scales::comma(n), ")")),
    vjust = -0.5
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Policies with vs without Claims",
    x = "Has Claim",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = comma,
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")








Interpretation: Most policies in the portfolio do not have any claims. Around 88.1% of policies have no claims, while only 11.9% have at least one claim. This indicates that claim occurrence is relatively rare, which is typical in motor insurance data. The strong imbalance also explains why the total_incurred variable has many zero values and a highly right-skewed distribution. For later modelling or statistical analysis, this imbalance should be considered carefully, especially if has_claim is used as a target variable.
This supports treating claim analysis as two parts: claim frequency and claim severity. claims are rare, data is imbalanced, and claim cost is zero-inflated because most policies have no claims.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Technical Report)
text:
Part 2: Bivariate Visual Analytics

Factors Influencing Profitability

Premium vs Claims Relationship

set.seed(123)

cor_value <- cor(
  insurance_clean$total_premium,
  insurance_clean$total_incurred,
  method = "spearman",
  use = "complete.obs"
)

insurance_clean %>%
  sample_n(min(5000, nrow(insurance_clean))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = total_premium, y = total_incurred)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "red", se = TRUE) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Premium vs Claim Cost Relationship",
    subtitle = paste0("Spearman correlation: ", round(cor_value, 3)),
    x = "Premium (€)",
    y = "Claim Cost (€)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()








Interpretation: Premium and claim cost have only a weak positive relationship. The Spearman correlation is 0.217, meaning higher-premium policies tend to have slightly higher claim costs, but the relationship is not strong.
I will keep the postitve claims only, and take a look at the relationship. Still, the Spearman correlation is 0.14, quite weak.

claims_positive <- insurance_clean %>%
  filter(total_incurred > 0)

cor_positive <- cor(
  claims_positive$total_premium,
  claims_positive$total_incurred,
  method = "spearman",
  use = "complete.obs"
)

claims_positive %>%
  sample_n(min(5000, nrow(claims_positive))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = total_premium, y = total_incurred)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "red", se = TRUE) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Premium vs Claim Cost Relationship among Claim Policies",
    subtitle = paste0("Positive claims only; Spearman correlation: ", round(cor_positive, 3)),
    x = "Premium (€)",
    y = "Claim Cost (€)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()










Profitability by Claim Occurrence

ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_clean,
  x = has_claim,
  y = profitability,
  type = "nonparametric",
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  p.adjust.method = "bonferroni",
  title = "Profitability Distribution by Claim Occurrence"
)








Interpretation: Claim occurrence shows the strongest relationship with profitability among all factors analyzed. The Mann-Whitney test reveals a highly significant difference (p < 0.001) with a large effect size. Policies without claims (No) have a median profitability of approximately €248, while policies with at least one claim (Yes) have dramatically lower median profitability, often negative or near zero. This confirms that claim occurrence is the most critical driver of profitability in the portfolio.
The effect size is large, with r≈0.78, indicating that claim occurrence is one of the strongest factors associated with profitability. Unlike some previous variables where the statistical significance was mainly driven by the large sample size, the difference here is both statistically and practically meaningful.

insurance_clean %>%
  filter(profitability >= -2000, profitability <= 2000) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = has_claim, y = profitability, fill = has_claim)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.7, trim = TRUE) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.5, outlier.alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Profitability by Claim Occurrence",
    subtitle = "Filtered to profitability between -€2,000 and €2,000 for readability",
    x = "Has Claim",
    y = "Profitability (€)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")








Interpretation: The violin plot clearly shows the dramatic difference in profitability distributions between policies with and without claims. Policies without claims are concentrated in positive profitability territory, with most values above zero. In contrast, policies with claims show a much wider distribution with substantial mass in negative profitability territory, indicating that claims directly erode profitability. This visual confirms that claim occurrence is the primary determinant of policy-level profitability.


Profitability by Fuel Type
Since profitability is highly non-normal and contains extreme outliers, a non-parametric approach was selected. The Kruskal-Wallis test was used instead of one-way ANOVA because it does not require the normality assumption and is more appropriate for comparing profitability distributions across fuel types.

ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_clean,
  x = fuel_type,
  y = profitability,
  type = "nonparametric",
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  p.adjust.method = "bonferroni",
  title = "Profitability Distribution by Fuel Type"
)








Interpretation: The Mann-Whitney test shows a statistically significant difference in profitability between the two fuel types, with a very small p-value. Diesel (D) policies have a higher median profitability of approximately €240.75, compared with €216.22 for gasoline (G) policies. This suggests that diesel policies are typically slightly more profitable than gasoline policies.However, the effect size is small (r ≈ 0.08), meaning the practical difference between the two groups is limited despite being statistically significant. This is likely because the dataset is very large, so even small differences can become statistically significant.
=> Fuel type is statistically associated with profitability, but the practical effect appears small.

insurance_clean %>%
  filter(profitability >= -2000, profitability <= 2000) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = fuel_type, y = profitability, fill = fuel_type)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.alpha = 0.25) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "Profitability by Fuel Type",
    subtitle = "Filtered to profitability between -€2,000 and €2,000 for readability",
    x = "Fuel Type",
    y = "Profitability (€)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")










Profitability by Region

ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_clean,
  x = circulation_area,
  y = profitability,
  type = "nonparametric",
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  p.adjust.method = "bonferroni",
  title = "Profitability Distribution by Circulation Area"
)








Interpretation: Policies in area U show slightly higher median profitability than those in area R. The difference is statistically significant, but the effect size is very small, indicating that circulation area alone does not strongly explain profitability differences.
=> Area matters statistically, but the practical difference is very small.


Profitability by Vehicle Age

ggplot(insurance_clean, aes(x = vehicle_age_group, y = profitability, fill = vehicle_age_group)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(title = "Profitability by Vehicle Age Group",
       x = "Vehicle Age (years)", y = "Profitability (€)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")








Interpretation: Profitability varies across vehicle age groups, but the chart is strongly influenced by extreme negative outliers. Most policies in all age groups have profitability clustered close to zero, suggesting that typical profitability differences are not very large. However, older vehicle groups show more severe downside outcomes, with larger negative profitability outliers. This pattern is especially visible for vehicles aged 41–50 years and 50+ years, suggesting that very old vehicles may be associated with higher claim severity and greater loss risk. Therefore, older vehicles appear to carry greater downside risk, even if the majority of observations remain close to zero.

insurance_clean %>%
  filter(
    profitability >= -2000,
    profitability <= 2000,
    !is.na(vehicle_age_group)
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(
    x = vehicle_age_group,
    y = profitability,
    fill = vehicle_age_group
  )) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.7, trim = TRUE) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.5, outlier.alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Profitability by Vehicle Age Group",
    subtitle = "Filtered to profitability between -€2,000 and €2,000 for readability",
    x = "Vehicle Age Group",
    y = "Profitability (€)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")








Interpretation: After filtering extreme profitability values, most vehicle age groups have similar median profitability, generally slightly above zero. However, older vehicle groups, especially 41-50 years and 50+ years, show wider negative tails, suggesting greater downside risk. This indicates that while typical profitability is relatively stable across vehicle age groups, very old vehicles may be more exposed to severe loss outcomes.


Profitability by Driver Age Group

ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_clean,
  x = driver_age_group,
  y = profitability,
  type = "nonparametric",
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  p.adjust.method = "bonferroni",
  title = "Profitability Distribution by Driver Age Group"
)








Interpretation: Profitability differs significantly across driver age groups based on the Kruskal-Wallis test, p<0.001. Younger drivers aged 18-25 show the highest median profitability, while the 36-50 and 65+ groups have lower median profitability. However, the effect size is very small, meaning the practical difference across age groups is limited. The result should also be interpreted carefully because the 18-25 group has a much smaller sample size than the other groups.


Profitability by Driving Experience Group

ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_clean,
  x = driving_experience_group,
  y = profitability,
  type = "nonparametric",
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  p.adjust.method = "bonferroni",
  title = "Profitability Distribution by Driving Experience Group"
)








Interpretation: Interpretation: The Kruskal-Wallis test shows a statistically significant difference in profitability across driving experience groups, p<0.001. However, the effect size is extremely small, suggesting that driving experience explains only a very limited amount of the variation in profitability. Most groups have broadly similar median profitability, generally around €225–€237. The 0-10 years group has a much lower median profitability, but this group contains only 15 observations, so it should not be used for strong conclusions. Overall, driving experience appears to have a statistically detectable but practically weak relationship with profitability.
Several groups also contain large negative profitability outliers, especially the 11-20 years, 21-30 years, and 31-40 years groups. This suggests that severe loss cases occur across different experience levels, not only among inexperienced drivers.


Profitability by Municipality Type

ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_clean,
  x = municipality_type,
  y = profitability,
  type = "nonparametric",
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  p.adjust.method = "bonferroni",
  title = "Profitability Distribution by Municipality Type"
)








Interpretation: The Kruskal-Wallis test shows a statistically significant difference in profitability across municipality types, p<0.001. Coastal municipalities (C) have the highest median profitability at approximately €239.97, followed by inland municipalities (I) at €226.89, and island municipalities (IS) at €218.29. This suggests that profitability varies by geographic classification.
However, the effect size is very small, meaning that municipality type explains only a limited amount of profitability variation. The statistical significance is likely partly driven by the very large sample size. In addition, the island group has a much smaller sample size than the coastal and inland groups, so its results should be interpreted more cautiously.


Profitability by Bonus Score

ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_clean,
  x = bonus_score,
  y = profitability,
  type = "nonparametric",
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  p.adjust.method = "bonferroni",
  title = "Profitability Distribution by Bonus Score"
)








Interpretation: The Kruskal-Wallis test shows a statistically significant difference in profitability across bonus score groups, p<0.001. The B group has the highest median profitability at approximately €333.29, followed by N at €239.25 and G at €228.79. This result is somewhat unexpected because B represents a poorer claims history, so it may have been expected to show lower profitability.
However, the effect size is very small, suggesting that bonus score explains only a limited amount of profitability variation. In addition, the dataset is highly imbalanced: the G group dominates the portfolio, while B and N have much smaller sample sizes. Therefore, although the statistical test is significant, the practical importance of the difference should be interpreted carefully.


Profitability by Policy Type

ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_clean,
  x = policy_type,
  y = profitability,
  type = "nonparametric",
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  p.adjust.method = "bonferroni",
  title = "Profitability Distribution by Policy Type"
)








Interpretation: The Kruskal-Wallis test shows a statistically significant difference in profitability across policy types, p<0.001. Among the groups, COMP_N has the highest median profitability at approximately €405.38, followed by COMP_E at €306.63. In contrast, TP has the lowest median profitability at approximately €150.08, while CC and TPG show moderate median profitability values.
=> This suggests that comprehensive policies, especially comprehensive policies without excess (COMP_N), tend to be more profitable in terms of median profitability. This may be because these policies generate higher premiums, which can offset claim costs. On the other hand, basic third-party policies (TP) appear less profitable, possibly because they have lower premium income while still being exposed to liability claims.
=> However, although the effect size is larger than in some previous categorical comparisons, it is still relatively small. This means policy type has a statistically significant association with profitability, but it does not fully explain profitability variation. Extreme negative outliers are also present across several policy types, especially COMP_E, indicating that severe loss cases can occur even in generally profitable product groups.


Power-to-Weight Ratio vs Profitability

set.seed(123)
cor_pwr <- cor(insurance_clean$power_to_weight_ratio, insurance_clean$profitability,
               method = "spearman", use = "complete.obs")

insurance_clean %>%
  sample_n(min(5000, nrow(insurance_clean))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = power_to_weight_ratio, y = profitability)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25, color = "purple") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "red", se = TRUE) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Power-to-Weight Ratio vs Profitability",
    subtitle = paste0("Spearman correlation: ", round(cor_pwr, 3)),
    x = "Power-to-Weight Ratio",
    y = "Profitability (€)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()








Interpretation: The scatter plot shows a weak negative relationship between weight-to-power ratio and profitability. The Spearman correlation is -0.107, suggesting that vehicles with higher weight-to-power ratios tend to have slightly lower profitability. However, the relationship is weak, and most observations are clustered close to zero profitability. This means weight-to-power ratio alone does not strongly explain profitability variation.


Total Exposure vs Profitability

set.seed(123)
cor_exp <- cor(insurance_clean$total_exposure, insurance_clean$profitability,
               method = "spearman", use = "complete.obs")

insurance_clean %>%
  sample_n(min(5000, nrow(insurance_clean))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = total_exposure, y = profitability)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25, color = "orange") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "red", se = TRUE) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Total Exposure vs Profitability",
    subtitle = paste0("Spearman correlation: ", round(cor_exp, 3)),
    x = "Total Exposure (years)",
    y = "Profitability (€)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()








Interpretation: The scatter plot shows a moderate positive relationship between total_exposure and profitability, with a Spearman correlation of 0.565. This suggests that policies with longer exposure periods tend to have higher profitability. This is reasonable because higher exposure generally means the policy was active for longer, allowing more premium to be earned.
However, total_exposure should be interpreted carefully because it is not a normal risk factor. It directly affects both premium earned and claim opportunity. Longer exposure can increase premium income, but it also gives more time for claims to occur. Therefore, the positive relationship may partly reflect the way profitability is constructed, rather than a pure risk effect.


Raincloud Plot: Profitability by Area

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggdist)
library(scales)

# Data for plotting only
plot_data <- insurance_clean %>%
  filter(profitability >= -2000, profitability <= 2000)

# Sample points for cleaner display
set.seed(123)
point_data <- plot_data %>%
  group_by(circulation_area) %>%
  slice_sample(n = 1500) %>%
  ungroup()

# Median values
median_data <- plot_data %>%
  group_by(circulation_area) %>%
  summarise(
    median_profit = median(profitability, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = circulation_area, y = profitability,
                      fill = circulation_area, color = circulation_area)) +
  
  # Half-eye / half-violin density
  stat_halfeye(
    adjust = 0.7,
    width = 0.6,
    justification = -0.25,
    .width = 0,
    point_colour = NA,
    alpha = 0.45
  ) +
  
  # Boxplot in the middle
  geom_boxplot(
    width = 0.12,
    outlier.shape = NA,
    alpha = 0.5,
    color = "black"
  ) +
  
  # Sampled individual points
  geom_jitter(
    data = point_data,
    width = 0.08,
    alpha = 0.10,
    size = 0.6,
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  
  # Median point
  geom_point(
    data = median_data,
    aes(x = circulation_area, y = median_profit),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    color = "black",
    size = 2.5
  ) +
  
  # Zero-profit line
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Raincloud Plot: Profitability by Circulation Area",
    subtitle = "Filtered to profitability between -€2,000 and €2,000 for readability",
    x = "Circulation Area",
    y = "Profitability (€)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )








Interpretation: The raincloud plot compares profitability between circulation areas R and U. Both areas show similar profitability distributions, with most observations concentrated slightly above zero. Area U appears to have a slightly higher median profitability than area R, which is consistent with the earlier Mann-Whitney result. However, the difference is visually small, suggesting that circulation area has limited practical impact on profitability. Both groups also show negative profitability cases, indicating that loss-making policies exist in both regions.
This visual result is consistent with the Mann-Whitney test, which found a statistically significant difference between areas. However, the effect size was very small, so the difference is statistically detectable mainly because of the large sample size rather than because of a strong practical difference.



Factors Influencing Profitability

Uncertainty Visualization: Mean Profitability with Confidence Intervals

profit_summary <- insurance_clean %>%
  group_by(fuel_type) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    median = median(profitability, na.rm = TRUE),
    q1 = quantile(profitability, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    q3 = quantile(profitability, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(n > 1000)

ggplot(profit_summary) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      x = reorder(fuel_type, median),
      ymin = q1,
      ymax = q3
    ),
    width = 0.2,
    colour = "black"
  ) +
  geom_point(
    aes(x = reorder(fuel_type, median), y = median),
    color = "red",
    size = 2
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Median Profitability by Fuel Type with Interquartile Range",
    subtitle = "Error bars represent the 25th to 75th percentile range",
    x = "Fuel Type",
    y = "Median Profitability (€)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()








Interpretation: Since profitability is highly skewed and contains extreme outliers, median profitability with interquartile range is used instead of mean profitability with confidence intervals. The median provides a more robust measure of central tendency, while the IQR shows the spread of the middle 50% of observations. This approach is more consistent with the non-parametric tests used earlier.


Claims Volatility Analysis

volatility_by_fuel <- insurance_clean %>%
  group_by(fuel_type) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_claim = mean(total_incurred, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_claim = sd(total_incurred, na.rm = TRUE),
    cv = sd_claim / mean_claim,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(n > 100)

ggplot(volatility_by_fuel, aes(x = reorder(fuel_type, cv), y = cv, fill = fuel_type)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(cv, 2)), hjust = -0.2) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = comma,
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Claims Volatility by Fuel Type",
    subtitle = "Coefficient of Variation (SD / Mean)",
    x = "Fuel Type",
    y = "Coefficient of Variation"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")








Interpretation: Claim cost volatility differs across fuel types. Gasoline (G) has the highest coefficient of variation, followed by diesel (D), suggesting that claim costs are more variable relative to their average claim amount for these two fuel types. Overall, claim costs appear highly volatile, especially for gasoline and diesel policies.
BUT Because many policies have zero claims, the coefficient of variation may be inflated. Therefore, this result should be interpreted as a relative volatility indicator rather than a complete measure of claim risk.


Volatility by Geographic Area

volatility_by_area <- insurance_clean %>%
  group_by(circulation_area) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_claim = mean(total_incurred, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_claim = sd(total_incurred, na.rm = TRUE),
    cv = sd_claim / mean_claim,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(volatility_by_area, aes(
  x = reorder(circulation_area, cv),
  y = cv,
  fill = circulation_area
)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(cv, 2)), hjust = -0.2) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = comma,
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Claims Volatility by Circulation Area",
    subtitle = "Coefficient of Variation (SD / Mean)",
    x = "Area",
    y = "Coefficient of Variation"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")








Interpretation: Claim cost volatility differs between circulation areas. Area R has a higher coefficient of variation than area U, indicating that claim costs in area R are more variable relative to their average claim amount. In contrast, area U shows lower relative claim volatility. This suggests that although both regions contain claim risk, area R may have less predictable or more dispersed claim costs.
However, because claim costs are zero-inflated, with many policies having no claims, the coefficient of variation may be inflated when the mean claim cost is low. Therefore, the CV should be interpreted as a relative volatility indicator rather than a complete measure of claim risk.


Profitability by Claim Occurrence

ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_clean,
  x = has_claim,
  y = profitability,
  type = "nonparametric",
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  p.adjust.method = "bonferroni",
  title = "Profitability Distribution by Claim Occurrence"
)








Interpretation: Policies with claims have much lower profitability than policies without claims, which is expected because profitability is calculated by subtracting incurred claims from premium. Therefore, this result should be treated as a validation check rather than a new insight. More meaningful analysis should focus on pre-claim variables that may help explain why some policies become less profitable.

title: Take-home Exercise 1 (Technical Report)
text:
Conclusion

Key Findings
Univariate Insights:

Premium is right-skewed, with most policies below €500 and a small number of high-premium outliers extending beyond €5,000.
Claim costs are highly zero-inflated — 88.1% of policies have no claims. Among those with claims, the distribution is strongly right-skewed, with a few extreme cases reaching nearly €600,000.
Profitability is generally positive for most policies, but the distribution has a long negative left tail driven by high-severity claims. The signed log transformation reveals two distinct clusters: a large profitable group and a smaller loss-making group.
Vehicle age is concentrated between 10–45 years, with relatively few new or very old vehicles. Driver age and driving experience are broadly distributed, with most policyholders in the 36–65 age range and 11–40 years of experience.
The portfolio is dominated by diesel vehicles (63.9%) and CC policy type (57.9%). Most policyholders have a good claims history (bonus score G).

Bivariate Insights:

Premium and claim costs have only a weak positive relationship (Spearman ρ = 0.217), indicating that premium alone does not strongly predict claim cost.
Total exposure has the strongest positive relationship with profitability (Spearman ρ = 0.565), reflecting that longer-active policies earn more premium. This should be interpreted carefully as exposure directly affects how profitability is constructed.
Power-to-weight ratio shows a weak negative relationship with profitability (Spearman ρ = −0.107), suggesting higher-performance vehicles carry slightly more risk.
Policy type shows the most meaningful group difference: comprehensive policies (COMP_N, COMP_E) have higher median profitability than basic third-party policies (TP), likely due to higher premium income.
Bonus score shows a counterintuitive pattern — the B (poor claims history) group has the highest median profitability — but this group is very small and results should be interpreted cautiously.
Profitability differs significantly across fuel type, circulation area, vehicle age group, driver age group, driving experience group, and municipality type. However, effect sizes are consistently very small across all these variables, meaning no single factor strongly explains profitability variation.
Policies with claims have substantially lower profitability than those without, which is expected by construction. This confirms the importance of claim occurrence as a driver of loss, rather than being a new analytical insight.

Volatility Findings:

Gasoline vehicles show the highest claim cost volatility (CV), followed by diesel, indicating greater unpredictability in claim amounts for these fuel types.
Area R has higher relative claim volatility than Area U, suggesting more dispersed or unpredictable claim costs in rural/regional areas.
All volatility measures are inflated by zero-inflation in claim costs and should be treated as relative risk indicators rather than absolute measures.



Recommendations

Policy Type Pricing: Comprehensive policies (COMP_N, COMP_E) are more profitable than basic third-party policies. Review whether TP pricing adequately compensates for liability exposure.
Claim Severity Management: A small number of extreme claims drive most losses. Consider differentiated coverage terms, claim monitoring, or reinsurance for high-severity segments.
Vehicle Age Risk: Older vehicles (41–50 years, 50+) show wider negative profitability tails. Underwriting criteria for very old vehicles should reflect this higher downside risk.
Geographic Volatility: Area R shows higher claim volatility than Area U. Pricing and reserving assumptions should account for this regional disparity.
Exposure-Adjusted Analysis: Since total exposure strongly correlates with profitability by construction, future analysis should use exposure-adjusted metrics (e.g. profitability per unit exposure) for fairer segment comparisons.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
In this chapter, you will learn the basic principles and essential components of ggplot2. At the same time, you will gain hands-on experience on using these components to plot statistical graphics based on the principle of Layered Grammar of Graphics. By then end of this chapter you will be able to apply the essential graphical elements provided by ggplot2 to create elegant and yet functional statistical graphics.






pacman::p_load(tidyverse)





exam_data <- read_csv("../data/Exam_data.csv")

Year end examination grades of a cohort of primary 3 students from a local school.
There are a total of seven attributes. Four of them are categorical data type and the other three are in continuous data type.
The categorical attributes are: ID, CLASS, GENDER and RACE. The continuous attributes are: MATHS, ENGLISH and SCIENCE.






R Graphics

hist(exam_data$MATHS)








ggplot

ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=10, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="black", 
                 fill="grey") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")








As you can see that the code chunk is relatively simple if R Graphics is used. Then, the question is why ggplot2 is recommended?
IMPORTANT: The transferable skills from ggplot2 are not the idiosyncrasies of plotting syntax, but a powerful way of thinking about visualisation, as a way of mapping between variables and the visual properties of geometric objects that you can perceive.




In the nutshell, Grammar of Graphics defines the rules of structuring mathematical and aesthetic elements into a meaningful graph.
There are two principles in Grammar of Graphics, they are:
Graphics = distinct layers of grammatical elements Meaningful plots through aesthetic mapping A good grammar of graphics will allow us to gain insight into the composition of complicated graphics, and reveal unexpected connections between seemingly different graphics (Cox 1978). It also provides a strong foundation for understanding a diverse range of graphics. Furthermore, it may also help guide us on what a well-formed or correct graphic looks like, but there will still be many grammatically correct but nonsensical graphics.


A short description of each building block are as follows:

Data: The dataset being plotted.
Aesthetics: Take attributes of the data and use them to influence visual characteristics, such as position, colours, size, shape, or transparency.
Geometrics: The visual elements used for our data, such as point, bar or line.
Facets: Split the data into subsets to create multiple variations of the same graph (paneling, multiple plots).
Statistics: Statistical transformations that summarise data (e.g. mean, confidence intervals).
Coordinate systems: Define the plane on which data are mapped on the graphic.
Themes: Modify all non-data components of a plot, such as main title, sub-title, y-axis title, or legend background.






ggplot(data=exam_data)








A blank canvas appears. ggplot() initializes a ggplot object. The data argument defines the dataset to be used for plotting. If the dataset is not already a data.frame, it will be converted to one by fortify().



The aesthetic mappings take attributes of the data and and use them to influence visual characteristics, such as position, colour, size, shape, or transparency. Each visual characteristic can thus encode an aspect of the data and be used to convey information.
All aesthetics of a plot are specified in the aes() function call (in later part of this lesson, you will see that each geom layer can have its own aes specification)
Code chunk below adds the aesthetic element into the plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS))











Geometric objects are the actual marks we put on a plot. Examples include:

geom_point for drawing individual points (e.g., a scatter plot)
geom_line for drawing lines (e.g., for a line chart)
geom_smooth for drawing smoothed lines (e.g., for simple trends or approximations)
geom_bar for drawing bars (e.g., for bar charts)
geom_histogram for drawing binned values (e.g., a histogram)
geom_polygon for drawing arbitrary shapes
geom_map for drawing polygons in the shape of a map (use map_data() to access map data)

A plot must have at least one geom; there is no upper limit. You can add a geom to a plot using the + operator.



ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()











In a dot plot, the width of a dot corresponds to the bin width (or maximum width, depending on the binning algorithm), and dots are stacked, with each dot representing one observation.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(dotsize = 0.5)








The y scale is not very useful, in fact it is very misleading.
The code chunk below performs the following two steps:
scale_y_continuous() is used to turn off the y-axis, and binwidth argument is used to change the binwidth to 2.5.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(binwidth=2.5,         
               dotsize = 0.5) +      
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,           
                     breaks = NULL)  











In the code chunk below, geom_histogram() is used to create a simple histogram by using values in MATHS field of exam_data.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram()       











In the code chunk below,
bins argument is used to change the number of bins to 20, fill argument is used to shade the histogram with light blue color, and color argument is used to change the outline colour of the bars in black

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20,            
                 color="black",      
                 fill="light blue")  











The code chunk below changes the interior colour of the histogram (i.e. fill) by using sub-group of aesthetic().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           fill = GENDER)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 color="grey30")











geom-density() computes and plots kernel density estimate, which is a smoothed version of the histogram.
It is a useful alternative to the histogram for continuous data that comes from an underlying smooth distribution.
The code below plots the distribution of Maths scores in a kernel density estimate plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_density()           








The code chunk below plots two kernel density lines by using colour or fill arguments of aes()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, 
           colour = GENDER)) +
  geom_density()











geom_boxplot() displays continuous value list. It visualises five summary statistics (the median, two hinges and two whiskers), and all “outlying” points individually.
The code chunk below plots boxplots by using geom_boxplot().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS,       
           x= GENDER)) +    
  geom_boxplot()            








Notches are used in box plots to help visually assess whether the medians of distributions differ. If the notches do not overlap, this is evidence that the medians are different.
The code chunk below plots the distribution of Maths scores by gender in notched plot instead of boxplot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot(notch=TRUE)











geom_violin is designed for creating violin plot. Violin plots are a way of comparing multiple data distributions. With ordinary density curves, it is difficult to compare more than just a few distributions because the lines visually interfere with each other. With a violin plot, it’s easier to compare several distributions since they’re placed side by side.
The code below plot the distribution of Maths score by gender in violin plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_violin()











geom_point() is especially useful for creating scatterplot.
The code chunk below plots a scatterplot showing the Maths and English grades of pupils by using geom_point().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point()            











The code chunk below plots the data points on the boxplots by using both geom_boxplot() and geom_point().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +                    
  geom_point(position="jitter", 
             size = 0.5)        












The Statistics functions statistically transform data, usually as some form of summary. For example:

frequency of values of a variable (bar graph)
a mean
a confidence limit

There are two ways to use these functions:

add a stat_() function and override the default geom, or
add a geom_() function and override the default stat.



The boxplots below are incomplete because the positions of the means were not shown.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot()











The code chunk below adds mean values by using stat_summary() function and overriding the default geom.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  stat_summary(geom = "point",       
               fun = "mean",         
               colour ="red",        
               size=4)               











The code chunk below adding mean values by using geom_() function and overriding the default stat.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_point(stat="summary",        
             fun="mean",           
             colour="red",          
             size=4)          











The scatterplot below shows the relationship of Maths and English grades of pupils. The interpretability of this graph can be improved by adding a best fit curve.
In the code chunk below, geom_smooth() is used to plot a best fit curve on the scatterplot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(linewidth=0.5)








The default method used is loess.
The default smoothing method can be overridden as shown below.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              linewidth=0.5)












Facetting generates small multiples (sometimes also called trellis plot), each displaying a different subset of the data. They are an alternative to aesthetics for displaying additional discrete variables. ggplot2 supports two types of factes, namely: facet_grid() and facet_wrap.


facet_wrap wraps a 1d sequence of panels into 2d. This is generally a better use of screen space than facet_grid because most displays are roughly rectangular.
The code chunk below plots a trellis plot using facet-wrap()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
    facet_wrap(~ CLASS)











facet_grid() forms a matrix of panels defined by row and column facetting variables. It is most useful when you have two discrete variables, and all combinations of the variables exist in the data.
The code chunk below plots a trellis plot using facet_grid().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
    facet_grid(~ CLASS)












The Coordinates functions map the position of objects onto the plane of the plot. There are a number of different possible coordinate systems to use, they are:

coord_cartesian(): the default cartesian coordinate systems, where you specify x and y values (e.g. allows you to zoom in or out).
coord_flip(): a cartesian system with the x and y flipped.
coord_fixed(): a cartesian system with a “fixed” aspect ratio (e.g. 1.78 for a “widescreen” plot).
coord_quickmap(): a coordinate system that approximates a good aspect ratio for maps.



By the default, the bar chart of ggplot2 is in vertical form.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()








The code chunk below flips the horizontal bar chart into vertical bar chart by using coord_flip().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip()











The scatterplot on the right is slightly misleading because the y-aixs and x-axis range are not equal.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5)








The code chunk below fixed both the y-axis and x-axis range from 0-100.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm,
              linewidth=0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))












Themes control elements of the graph not related to the data. For example:

background colour
size of fonts
gridlines
colour of labels

Built-in themes include: theme_gray() (default), theme_bw(), theme_classic()



ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_gray()








A horizontal bar chart plotted using theme_classic().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_classic()








A horizontal bar chart plotted using theme_minimal().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal()

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text: In this chapter, you will learn the basic principles and essential components of ggplot2. At the same time, you will gain hands-on experience on using these components to plot statistical graphics based on the principle of Layered Grammar of Graphics. By then end of this chapter you will be able to apply the essential graphical elements provided by ggplot2 to create elegant and yet functional statistical graphics.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
pacman::p_load(tidyverse)





exam_data <- read_csv("../data/Exam_data.csv")

Year end examination grades of a cohort of primary 3 students from a local school.
There are a total of seven attributes. Four of them are categorical data type and the other three are in continuous data type.
The categorical attributes are: ID, CLASS, GENDER and RACE. The continuous attributes are: MATHS, ENGLISH and SCIENCE.

  geom_histogram(bins=10, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="black", 
                 fill="grey") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")








As you can see that the code chunk is relatively simple if R Graphics is used. Then, the question is why ggplot2 is recommended?
IMPORTANT: The transferable skills from ggplot2 are not the idiosyncrasies of plotting syntax, but a powerful way of thinking about visualisation, as a way of mapping between variables and the visual properties of geometric objects that you can perceive.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
In the nutshell, Grammar of Graphics defines the rules of structuring mathematical and aesthetic elements into a meaningful graph.
There are two principles in Grammar of Graphics, they are:
Graphics = distinct layers of grammatical elements Meaningful plots through aesthetic mapping A good grammar of graphics will allow us to gain insight into the composition of complicated graphics, and reveal unexpected connections between seemingly different graphics (Cox 1978). It also provides a strong foundation for understanding a diverse range of graphics. Furthermore, it may also help guide us on what a well-formed or correct graphic looks like, but there will still be many grammatically correct but nonsensical graphics.


A short description of each building block are as follows:

Data: The dataset being plotted.
Aesthetics: Take attributes of the data and use them to influence visual characteristics, such as position, colours, size, shape, or transparency.
Geometrics: The visual elements used for our data, such as point, bar or line.
Facets: Split the data into subsets to create multiple variations of the same graph (paneling, multiple plots).
Statistics: Statistical transformations that summarise data (e.g. mean, confidence intervals).
Coordinate systems: Define the plane on which data are mapped on the graphic.
Themes: Modify all non-data components of a plot, such as main title, sub-title, y-axis title, or legend background.
